text:
CT CHEST / ABDOMEN / PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
Second Restaging Examination

CLINICAL INDICATION: 62-year-old female with Stage IIIB right upper lobe pulmonary adenocarcinoma, EGFR exon 21 L858R mutation-positive. Currently on osimertinib (Tagrisso) 80 mg daily as first-line therapy, approximately 9 months since treatment initiation. Prior imaging at 3 months demonstrated significant partial response with reduction of the primary mass from 4.8 cm to 2.6 cm and interval decrease in mediastinal lymphadenopathy. This study obtained for continued disease monitoring. Comparison made to prior CT chest/abdomen/pelvis dated approximately 6 months prior (3-month restaging study) and to the baseline PET-CT at diagnosis.

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following the administration of 100 mL of Omnipaque 350 intravenous contrast. Images were obtained during portal venous phase for the abdomen and pelvis and during the post-contrast phase for the chest. Slice thickness: 1.25 mm reconstructed in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes. Lung windows, mediastinal windows, and soft tissue windows reviewed. Dose modulation utilized. No oral contrast administered.

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways:
The right upper lobe spiculated mass, previously measuring 2.6 cm in longest diameter on the 3-month restaging examination (and 4.8 cm at baseline), has continued to decrease in size and now measures approximately 1.8 cm in longest dimension (series 4, image 38). This represents a further reduction of approximately 31% compared to the 3-month scan and a cumulative reduction of roughly 62% from the pre-treatment baseline measurement. The lesion maintains a residual area of pleural tethering along the minor fissure and retains peripheral spiculation, though these findings are notably less pronounced than at baseline. The interface with the right upper lobe bronchus, which was abutted at initial staging, now appears more clearly delineated with no endobronchial component appreciated. There is a small zone of peritumoral ground-glass opacity surrounding the central solid component, consistent with a residual lepidic or inflammatory halo rather than frank tumor extension -- this finding is mildly less conspicuous compared to the prior study.

No new pulmonary nodules are identified in either lung. No satellite nodules in the right upper lobe. The remaining right lung fields demonstrate mild subsegmental atelectasis at the right lower lobe base, consistent with prior findings and possibly related to mild mucus plugging given the patient's background mild COPD. No consolidation. No new ground-glass opacities.

The left lung is clear. No focal mass or nodule identified. No consolidation.

The central airways are patent bilaterally. The trachea is midline. No endobronchial lesion is identified. The mainstem bronchi and segmental bronchi are widely patent.

Hyperinflation of bilateral upper lobes is noted, mildly increased in comparison to prior, consistent with the patient's known COPD. No significant new bullous disease. No evidence of air trapping on current examination. Mild centrilobular emphysema in the upper lobes bilaterally, unchanged.

Pleura:
No pleural effusion bilaterally. No pneumothorax. No pleural thickening. No pleural masses or nodules. The right pleural space is clear without evidence of loculated fluid.

Mediastinum and Hila:
The right paratracheal (station 4R) lymph node, previously measuring 0.9 cm on short-axis at the 3-month scan, now measures approximately 0.6 cm in short axis. This node has substantially reduced from the 1.8 cm short-axis measurement at the 3-month scan (and 1.8 cm short-axis at baseline staging). At its current size, this node is within normal limits and no longer meets conventional size criteria for pathologic lymphadenopathy.

The subcarinal lymph node (station 7), previously 0.7 cm at 3 months (reduced from 1.5 cm at baseline), now measures approximately 0.5 cm in short axis. This represents continued interval reduction and is within normal limits.

No new enlarged mediastinal or hilar lymph nodes. There is no evidence of new mediastinal adenopathy in the precarinal, prevascular, aortopulmonary window, or para-aortic stations. The left hilar and right hilar regions demonstrate no pathologically enlarged nodes.

The heart is normal in size. No pericardial effusion. The great vessels are patent. The aorta is normal in caliber throughout without evidence of aneurysmal dilatation or dissection. The pulmonary arteries are not enlarged. No central pulmonary embolism.

Chest Wall and Osseous Structures:
No suspicious lytic or sclerotic osseous lesions in the visualized thoracic spine, ribs, sternum, or shoulder girdles. Mild degenerative changes in the thoracic spine, most prominent at T7-T8 and T9-T10, unchanged. No pathologic fractures. No destructive lesions.

ABDOMEN:

Liver:
The liver is normal in size and morphology. Liver parenchyma enhances homogeneously without focal lesion, mass, or nodule identified. No hypervascular or hypovascular lesion suspicious for metastatic disease. No biliary ductal dilatation. The gallbladder is unremarkable; no cholelithiasis. No pericholecystic fluid.

Spleen:
Normal in size and attenuation. No focal splenic lesion.

Pancreas:
The pancreas is normal in morphology without mass or ductal abnormality. No pancreatic duct dilatation.

Adrenal Glands:
Both adrenal glands are normal in size and morphology. The right adrenal gland demonstrates a limb thickness measuring up to 5 mm, well within normal limits. The left adrenal gland is similarly unremarkable. No adrenal nodule or mass is identified. No hemorrhage. This is an important observation given the propensity of lung adenocarcinoma to metastasize to the adrenal glands; no such involvement is seen.

Kidneys and Ureters:
Both kidneys are normal in size and demonstrate symmetric nephrographic enhancement. A 5 mm simple cortical cyst is noted in the lower pole of the right kidney, unchanged from prior. No solid renal mass. No hydronephrosis. No urolithiasis identified on the non-contrast portions.

Gastrointestinal Tract:
The stomach, small bowel, and visualized large bowel loops are unremarkable without wall thickening, obstruction, or mass lesion. The appendix is not visualized (normal variant).

Retroperitoneum and Mesentery:
No retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy. The retroperitoneal vasculature is normal. The abdominal aorta is mildly tortuous but not aneurysmal.

PELVIS:

Bladder:
The urinary bladder is adequately distended and demonstrates no wall thickening, mass, or intraluminal filling defect.

Uterus and Ovaries:
The uterus is small and atrophic, appropriate for post-menopausal status. Bilateral ovaries are not well-visualized, as expected in a patient of this age. No pelvic mass identified.

Pelvic Lymph Nodes:
No pelvic or inguinal lymphadenopathy.

Osseous Structures of the Pelvis and Lumbar Spine:
No suspicious lytic or blastic osseous lesions in the visualized lumbar spine, sacrum, iliac bones, or proximal femora. Mild facet arthropathy in the lower lumbar spine. No pathologic fractures.

IMPRESSION:

1. Continued reduction in size of the right upper lobe primary pulmonary adenocarcinoma: the mass now measures approximately 1.8 cm in longest dimension, down from 2.6 cm at the 3-month restaging examination and markedly reduced from the 4.8 cm baseline measurement prior to initiation of osimertinib. The overall trajectory remains one of meaningful and sustained tumor regression now approaching the 9-month mark of therapy.

2. The right paratracheal (4R) lymph node has further diminished to 0.6 cm in short axis and is no longer enlarged. The subcarinal (station 7) lymph node measures 0.5 cm, similarly within normal size limits. There is no residual mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy to suggest persistent nodal disease by size criteria.

3. No new pulmonary nodules, no new mediastinal disease, and no new intrathoracic findings suspicious for disease progression or new sites of involvement.

4. No pleural effusion.

5. No evidence of hepatic, adrenal, or other intra-abdominal or pelvic metastatic disease. Adrenal glands remain unremarkable bilaterally.

6. No suspicious osseous lesions throughout the imaged skeleton.

7. Stable background mild COPD-related emphysematous changes bilaterally.

Overall, these findings are consistent with continued and deepening tumor response to osimertinib therapy. The primary lesion has more than halved from its size at the 3-month assessment and is now substantially reduced relative to the pre-treatment baseline, reflecting sustained ongoing disease control without evidence of new intrathoracic or extrathoracic disease. Correlation with clinical status and continued follow-up imaging recommended per treating oncology team's discretion.

Note: Intracranial evaluation is not included on this examination. Dedicated brain MRI was previously negative at initial staging; the need for interval brain imaging should be addressed separately by the treating team.

Electronically signed by:
Attending Radiologist, Thoracic Imaging Division
Department of Radiology
